title: NASA Mission Designer
description: You are a mission designer at JPL, working on trajectory design and mission planning for robotic exploration missions. Your current assignment is the trajectory design for a sample return mission to an asteroid. The work is deeply mathematical, the timescales are long (missions take 5-10 years from concept to launch), and the precision required is extraordinary — navigate a spacecraft billions of kilometres using gravity assists and arrive within metres of the target.

schedule.activity: Arrive at JPL. Coffee in Building 230. Check email for overnight trajectory optimisation runs — the Monte Python tool ran 2,000 trajectory variants overnight.
schedule.note: JPL's work-life balance is better than SpaceX, but mission deadlines still create crunch periods.

schedule.activity: Review optimisation results. Three trajectory options meet the delta-v budget with Earth and Venus gravity assists. Analyse the trade-offs: launch date flexibility vs propellant margin.

Mission design team meeting. Present trajectory options to the project scientist and systems engineer. The scientist wants a longer observation window at the asteroid; this changes the approach trajectory.
Work on the orbit determination plan — how will we track the spacecraft during cruise? Define the ground station schedule and navigation accuracy requirements.
Lunch in the JPL cafeteria. The Mars 2020 team is celebrating a successful Perseverance manoeuvre. Stories about the '7 minutes of terror' landing.
Deep work: model the asteroid proximity operations. Design the orbit around the asteroid for the sampling phase. Small body gravity is weak and irregular — this is a unique astrodynamics problem.
schedule.note: Asteroid operations require modelling irregular gravity fields — no simple Kepler orbits here.

schedule.activity: Peer review a colleague's Mars Sample Return trajectory. Check the Earth re-entry conditions — the capsule must hit a 0.2-degree entry corridor after a 2-year return cruise.
Write a section of the mission concept document for the upcoming proposal review. Clear technical writing is essential — proposals compete for funding.
Head home. Read a new paper on low-thrust trajectory optimisation on the train — always learning.

responsibilities: Design interplanetary trajectories using gravity assists and low-thrust propulsion
Perform orbit determination and navigation analysis
Support mission concept studies and proposal development
Develop and validate trajectory optimisation tools
Present technical results to project teams and review boards
challenges: Programme timescales are very long — 5-10 years from concept to launch
Funding is uncertain — missions can be cancelled after years of work
Navigation precision requirements are extreme (metres at billions of km distance)
Salary is lower than private sector for equivalent skill level
rewards: Working on missions that push the boundaries of human knowledge
Seeing your trajectory design carry a spacecraft to another world
Collaborating with the best astrodynamicists on the planet
Your work becomes part of space exploration history
